text: Three 5mm ports and one 12mm port are placed through the patient's previous surgical scars. The surgeon inspects the abdomen, finding no adhesions, and reflects the greater omentum cephalad. The jejuno-jejunostomy is identified, showing intermittent intussusception with the roux limb prolapsing into the common channel. The interopexy stitch is visible but ineffective.

The surgeon inspects and traces the small bowel limbs to their origins, identifying the Roux limb traveling through the mesocolon, the biliopancreatic limb emerging from the ligament of Treitz, and the common channel extending distally to the cecum. The transverse colon defect, Peterson space, and small bowel mesentery are closed, showing no signs of obstruction.

The surgeon resects the jejuno-jejunostomy, starting with the biliopancreatic limb using a 60mm endogia stapler with a 2.5mm staple cartridge. The biliopancreatic limb is marked with a single long suture for identification. The roux limb is transected similarly, proximal to the anastomosis, and marked with a double suture. The common limb is stapled off as well.

The surgeon transects the mesentery using vascular two millimeter reloads, setting the specimen aside for later removal in an endocatch bag. The roux limb, common limb, and biliopancreatic limb are visibly free from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon opens the small bowel mesenteric defect using a harmonic scalpel to further mobilize the biliopancreatic limb. A stay suture is placed, and a side-to-side functional end-to-end anastomosis is created between the roux limb and the common channel using a triple stapling technique. This involves two firings of an endo-GIA stapler with a 2.5 mm cartridge, each approximately 45 mm and positioned 180 degrees apart, followed by closure with a final 60 mm endo-GIA with a white load.

The surgeon closes the new mesenteric defect between the root and common limbs using a running 3-0 silk suture. Subsequently, the biliopancreatic limb is anastomosed to the common limb approximately ten centimeters distal to the initial anastomosis. The new mesenteric defect between the biliopancreatic limb and the common limb is also closed with a running 3-0 silk suture.

The surgeon verifies the anatomy of the retrocolic roux limb, tracing it to the common channel and anastomosis with the biliopancreatic limb, which is followed back to the ligament of Treitz. Both anastomoses are confirmed to be patent.
